common_name: Wild Coco (Wild Coco Orchid, Alta Eulophia, Haitian Orchid)

planting.note: Plant pseudobulbs or divisions in spring in partial shade to full sun in moderately moist, well-drained, sandy to loamy soil; Eulophia alta is one of the most adaptable terrestrial orchids for zone 9-11 outdoor cultivation — it naturally grows in disturbed sites, lawns, roadsides, and open woodlands and tolerates a wider range of soil and light conditions than most orchids. In zone 9, established plants survive mild winters; mulch in hard freeze years. Self-seeds and can naturalize as a 'lawn orchid' in warm, moist conditions. Divide clumps every 3–5 years. Non-toxic.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moderately moist, well-drained, loamy or sandy soil — Wild Coco is one of the most adaptable terrestrial orchids available, tolerating full sun to partial shade and a range of soil moisture levels, making it far easier to establish in zone 9-11 gardens than most orchids. Eulophia alta is a large, robust terrestrial orchid native to Florida (central and southern portions), the Caribbean, Mexico, Central America, South America, and tropical Africa in the Orchidaceae family — one of the most widely distributed of all terrestrial orchids, inhabiting an enormous range from Florida lawns to African grasslands. The plant produces large, clustered, corn-like pseudobulbs with 3–5 long, broad, pleated, bright-green leaves forming an impressive clump. In spring and summer, tall erect flower spikes (2–4 feet) emerge bearing 8–25 flowers in shades of brownish-purple on the outside with a pale pink to whitish, three-lobed lip — not the most brightly colored orchid, but perfectly attractive and interesting. The species has a remarkable ability to establish itself spontaneously in lawns and open ground in warm Florida and Gulf Coast areas, earning the informal title of 'lawn orchid.' For gardeners who want to add Florida-native orchids to outdoor garden beds without the demanding cultural requirements of most epiphytic orchids, E. alta is the most accessible starting point. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Eulophia alta: Florida's wild coco orchid
Florida Native Plant Society — Eulophia alta species profile
American Orchid Society — Eulophia alta cultivation notes
Florida Department of Environmental Protection — Native Orchids of Florida

common_name: Pine Pink (Purple Bletia, Rose Pogonia, Cuban Ground Orchid)

planting.note: Plant corms or pot-grown divisions in spring in partial shade to full sun in well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Bletia purpurea grows naturally in Florida's pine flatwoods and sandy, open habitats and needs good drainage rather than the consistently moist conditions favored by many orchids. In zone 9, established plants typically overwinter successfully with the corms protected by mulch. The plant goes partially dormant in winter. The vivid magenta-pink flowers are produced primarily in spring. Propagate by dividing corm clusters. Non-toxic.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in well-drained, sandy, lean soil — Pine Pink grows naturally in Florida's nutrient-poor, sandy pine flatwoods and scrub habitats and performs better in lean, well-drained soil than in rich, fertile garden beds. Bletia purpurea is a corm-producing terrestrial orchid native to Florida (central and southern), the Florida Keys, the Caribbean (Cuba, Hispaniola, Jamaica, Puerto Rico, the Lesser Antilles), Mexico, and Central America in the Orchidaceae family. The plant grows from clusters of corms (fleshy, rounded, underground storage organs) that produce 2–4 narrow, pleated, grass-like leaves and, in spring, one or more erect flower spikes 18–36 inches tall bearing 5–15 flowers in vivid, clear magenta-pink to bright rose-purple — one of the most vivid and cheerful flower colors available from a Florida-native terrestrial orchid. The bright pink flowers have a similar intensity and quality to a hardy Cattleya alliance hybrid, and the naturalness of their occurrence in Florida's sandy pinelands makes them particularly meaningful as a native plant. Pine Pink has demonstrated the ability to naturalize and spread in sandy, disturbed ground in Florida lawns and gardens — one of the few Florida native orchids that can be sustainably cultivated and propagated from legally sourced stock. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Bletia purpurea: Pine Pink orchid for Florida gardens
Florida Native Plant Society — Bletia purpurea species profile
American Orchid Society — Bletia purpurea outdoor cultivation guide
Florida Department of Environmental Protection — Native Orchids of Florida

common_name: Water Spider Orchid (Creeping Bog Orchid, Floating Orchid, Repen's Habenaria)

planting.note: Grow in shallow water (0–3 inch depth) or constantly saturated soil at pond edges, in rain gardens, or in containers of aquatic soil kept submerged; Habenaria repens is unique among orchids in that it genuinely thrives in aquatic and flooded conditions where most orchids would drown. The plant spreads by producing floating runners (stolons) that carry new plantlets — in a pond, these runners float across the surface and root wherever they contact soil. In zone 9, established colonies survive winters. Place where the delicate white flowers can be viewed at close range. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow in shallow water, saturated soil, or pond margins in partial shade to full sun — Water Spider Orchid is unique among cultivated orchids in tolerating and preferring genuinely wet to aquatic conditions, making it an excellent choice for rain gardens, pond edges, and water features. Habenaria repens is a small, semi-aquatic terrestrial orchid native to the southeastern United States (Florida, Georgia, Louisiana, Texas, North Carolina), the Caribbean, Mexico, Central America, and South America in the Orchidaceae family. It is one of the few orchids in the world genuinely adapted to aquatic and flooded environments — it grows naturally in shallow water, flooded ditches, pond margins, and wet prairies where standing water is normal, and spreads by producing floating stolons (horizontal runners) that carry small plantlets that root when they contact saturated soil or float to new locations. The plant produces upright stems of bright, fresh, green, succulent leaves. In summer and fall, the stems terminate in racemes of small (0.5-inch), intricate, bright white flowers with deeply fringed, spider-like petals — the fringed white petals arranged in a precise, delicate pattern that earned the common name 'Water Spider Orchid.' A fascinating and highly unusual orchid for water garden features in zone 8-12. Non-toxic.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Habenaria repens: Water Spider Orchid for Florida ponds
Florida Native Plant Society — Habenaria repens aquatic orchid species profile
American Orchid Society — Habenaria repens cultivation in water features
Florida Department of Environmental Protection — Native Orchids of Florida

common_name: Yellow Yam (Yellow Guinea Yam, Cayenne Yam, Yellow Cocoyam)

planting.note: Plant cut tuber sections ('setts') or small tubers in full sun after last frost in well-drained, deep, fertile, loamy to sandy soil; provide a sturdy vertical support (trellis, fence, or pole) as the vine will climb vigorously to 10–20 feet. Harvest when the vine dies back in late fall or early winter (240–300 days from planting). The distinctive yellow to deep-orange flesh (the 'yellow yam' characteristic) distinguishes D. cayennensis from white-fleshed yam species. Tubers must be cooked before eating (raw tubers contain toxic alkaloids). Cured tubers store for months. Zone 9-12 outdoor culture.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep, well-drained, fertile, loamy to sandy soil with a tall climbing support — Yellow Yam vines climb vigorously (10–20 feet) and require sturdy support throughout the growing season; deep, loose, well-worked soil allows the tubers to expand to their full size without impediment. Dioscorea cayennensis is a tuberous, annual to perennial twining vine native to West Africa and naturalized throughout the tropical world in the Dioscoreaceae family, one of the most important yam species in West African cuisine — the principal yam of the 'yam belt' that stretches across Ghana, Nigeria, Côte d'Ivoire, Benin, and Togo where yams are a dietary staple, ceremonially important, and economically critical. The distinctive feature of D. cayennensis is the yellow to deep orange-yellow flesh of the tubers — in contrast to the white-fleshed D. rotundata (white guinea yam), D. cayennensis tubers have the appearance of cooked sweet potato inside after cooking. The large (5–15 pound per tuber), cylindrical to irregularly shaped tubers are smooth-skinned and store well after curing. In zone 9-12 gardens, the vine grows vigorously through the warm months before dying back as days shorten, signaling tuber maturity. Raw tubers contain dioscorine and other toxic alkaloids — always cook before eating. One of the finest true yam species for Gulf Coast tropical food gardens.
sources: Food and Agriculture Organization — Dioscorea cayennensis: Yellow Guinea Yam
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical yams for Florida food gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tropical root vegetables for Gulf Coast zone 9-10
Purdue University NewCROP — Dioscorea species: edible yams

common_name: Chinese Yam (Asiatic Yam, Sweet Yam, Lesser Yam, Intoxicating Yam)

planting.note: Plant cut tuber sections or small tubers in full sun in deep, well-drained, fertile, sandy to loamy soil after last frost; Dioscorea esculenta produces multiple smaller tubers rather than one or two large ones — loosened, deep, fertile soil maximizes tuber development. Provide a tall climbing support. Unlike some other Dioscorea species, D. esculenta tubers may be eaten with the skin on after cooking. The tubers are sweet and have a distinctive, pleasant starchy flavor. Harvest when the vine begins to die back. Zone 9-12 outdoor production.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep, fertile, well-drained, sandy to loamy soil with a tall climbing support — Chinese Yam produces a vigorous twining vine that requires sturdy vertical support and rewards deep soil preparation with a generous yield of multiple smaller tubers around the central plant. Dioscorea esculenta is a tuberous, climbing vine native to Southeast Asia (India, Sri Lanka, Southeast Asia through Malaysia, Indonesia, and the Philippines) and widely cultivated across the Pacific Islands and tropical world in the Dioscoreaceae family. Unlike the large, single-tuber yam species (D. rotundata, D. cayennensis), D. esculenta produces clusters of multiple smaller, ovoid to elongated tubers 2–6 inches long, smooth to slightly warty, with white flesh and a naturally sweet, pleasant starchy flavor — one of the most palatable raw-flavor yam species and one that can be eaten simply baked, boiled, or roasted with a sweet, potato-like quality. The vines are typically spined at the base (small spines on the lower stems), climbing vigorously 10–15 feet per season. D. esculenta is the most widely grown yam species in the Pacific Islands and southern Asia, where it is valued both for its edible tubers and for the medicinal compounds in the leaves and skin. In zone 9-12 Gulf Coast and Florida gardens, it provides a productive, tropical root vegetable harvest from fall plantings or spring starts, with harvest occurring before the first frost in zone 9 or when the vine dies back naturally in zone 10-12.
sources: Food and Agriculture Organization — Dioscorea esculenta: Asiatic Yam
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical root vegetables for Florida food gardens
Hawaii Cooperative Extension Service — Dioscorea esculenta cultivation notes
Purdue University NewCROP — Dioscorea esculenta: lesser yam
